paragraphs.body: What surprised me the most in this class, as someone who has never seriously delved into creative writing before, is the idea that the best way to build a story is to start with the characters and then figure out the plot, rather than the other way around. I had always thought of the plot as being the most important thing, the thing that really gets the interest of the reader and turns a book into a real page-turner. But as I’ve been learning, you could have an amazing plot but if your characters are flat, inconsistent, and have unclear motivations, the story just won’t be that gripping. The suggestion of writing out your characters and figuring out the plot as you go along feels totally backwards to how I always thought of writing – for me, most of my writing in high school and college was in essay format where outlining and planning was strongly encouraged before you put pen to paper. Yet it really makes sense, in a way. If you want your characters to be interesting, convincing, they should make decisions and actions that they would actually do. The other way around, you might try to force your characters into boxes so that your plot can happen, but then the heart of the story just won’t be there.

annotations.comment: Yes, it’s a big change from what most of us have thought!
annotations.context_left: ild a story is to start with the characters and then figure out the plot, rather than the other way

annotations.comment: Sigh. I guess it makes sense but I tend to think essays also benefit from thinking on paper vs. preplanning.

paragraphs.body: In “Bird by Bird,” Anne Lamott quotes E. L. Doctorow who describes writing as similar to “driving a car at night – you can only see as far as your headlights, but you can make the whole trip that way.” It’s kind of a scary thought – I don’t really like driving at night for that reason. But it’s also comforting – you can always just keep going, and focus on the journey rather than the destination. Otherwise, you’ll be too worried about making the ending land and the plot work that you won’t allow your story to naturally develop on its own. In “The Semplica-Girl Diaries,” George Saunders spends a lot of time developing the narrator through his diary entries, often focusing on mundane and abstract thoughts he has, while the main plot of the “SG girls” is more of a background thought initially. As I was reading, I thus felt like I was really getting an understanding of the narrator and his character, and often found myself relating to him, which I think was intentional by Saunders.

annotations.context_right: spends a lot of time developing the narrator through his diary entries, often focusing on mundane a

paragraphs.body: Just in the first paragraph, we get a candid glance into the narrator’s psyche as he rambles about various things – “Last night dreamed of two demons having sex and found it was only two cats fighting outside window. Will future people be aware of concept of ‘demons’? Will they find our belief in ‘demons’ quaint? Will ‘windows’ even exist?” This is the sort of section that maybe, from a writer’s perspective, you aren’t necessarily thinking of when you first conceive of a story idea. It has nothing yet to do with the central intrigue of the SGs. Yet it’s still very important, and it’s in the beginning for a reason. This is your first look into the narrator’s character, and it establishes him as, first of all, a relatable person, because who hasn’t had these sorts of rambling thoughts before? But also, it shows that he’s not exactly in full control, he’s not all-knowing or super confident, he’s just a guy.

Maybe George Saunders always intended the narrator’s interactions with the SGs and his daughters to be the way it ended up – but I believe the nature of those interactions was partly determined by his initial characterization. His actions throughout the rest of the story follow believable motivations of a somewhat depressed middle aged man who feels somewhat unsatisfied with his status relative to others and wants to make his family feel proud – it’s not like his purchase of the SGs felt forced or out of character. While the concept of SGs is interesting (and horrifying) on its own, it’s the way the narrator and his daughters interact with the SGs that make this story so riveting, and really makes it “hit home” rather than being forgettable.

annotations.comment: Yeah, I know a little bit about his process and he does really let his characters lead him.

paragraphs.body: Another story I read from “Best American Short Stories (2020)” was “The Hands of Dirty Children.” This story is a sad one, following a group of homeless boys that call themselves the “Crazy 9” in Venezuela, particularly focusing on a narrator and his friend Ramoncito. This story, being from the perspective of a child, has a lot of work to do in terms of realistic characterization. Early on, there is a section where the narrator goes through a list of things that Crazy 9 love, each paragraphs starting with “We love...” and detailing the kinds of places they like to go to and things they like to do. This serves as a way to introduce us to these characters, and also really get into the mindset of these children. Even the use of the word “love”, which can be quite a strong word, shows their childish and carefree nature, and particularly the contrast of the things they love. The things they love include mangos, because of their sweetness and availability, but also the knife they stole from a lady they attempted to rob. Something I’ve learned throughout this course is that there could theoretically be a “realistic” way to write people, such as children, but really this is always going to vary based on the context of the story, and what’s important is having a story that works within itself and grips the reader. In this case, the author was going for characters that are childish, indeed, but have seen and experienced things due to their homelessness that are maybe beyond what they should. This opening characterization helps the rest of the story “work” – the meandering nature of the narrator as he walks with Ramoncito, as well as his sense of understanding when Ramoncito dies – this is a character that is young, carefree, but also understands loss and hardship, perhaps too well.

paragraphs.body: I think in a different story, the narrator’s actions and reactions might confuse me, because of how I might project my own thoughts of a child character onto him – I would be taken aback by his handling of Ramoncito’s dead body, because I know I would definitely be horribly traumatized if it happened to me at that age. But the way in which he and the children were initially described help give me context and understanding of why he reacted in this way. It didn’t feel like everything was just a setup for Ramoncito’s death – I felt invested in his character and emotionally impacted by the plight of the children at the end. Focusing on the characters first, rather than the plot, is a big reason why this story worked, in my opinion.

In my own work, I generally stuck with letting my characters do their thing before the plot really came into place fully. For my first story, I was actually inspired by something I saw on my way to this class itself – someone on a bike was yelling and desperately biking down Bigelow, seemingly after someone, and very nearly turned left without looking into oncoming traffic. I wanted to keep watching and see what was really going on, but unfortunately, I really did have to get to class. Seeing this gave me a starting off point for my story, giving an intriguing mystery, but also the beginnings of a characterization for the main character – he must be quite desperate, but also very reckless given his attempted turn with little hesitation. As I wrote, I expanded on this, and the idea of him hitting someone by mistake came to me before the idea of him hurting his girlfriend by mistake. So the plot developed somewhat naturally in that way, because his actions felt consistent to me based on how I described him initially. Obviously, not perfect, and the plot overall was a little forced and convenient. But I liked how it developed from pretty much nothing, and made me feel more confident in writing the same way in the future – not to worry about having some amazing plot outlined beforehand, and instead letting the characters speak and act for themselves.

annotations.comment: Definitely can see that. It’s a strength of your writing!
Love that. People often ask me where to find ideas for stories, and I think: just look around! Stay alert!
But that’s fine because it was a draft!

paragraphs.body: In the future, I don’t expect to become a professional writer. But should I pick up then figurative pen and paper again, these are the things I’ll be thinking about most. Not thinking about the destination first, but instead about the characters themselves. I’ve also found that my enjoyment of media, both literature and on screen has improved with this in mind. I’ve found that the stories I enjoy are the ones where there are meaningful, interesting interactions between characters that don’t just feel like filler or just there to move the plot. You want the reader to want to be like a fly on the wall – and writing is the perfect way to get that! That’s what can make literature and other forms of storytelling so fulfilling when done right. I find myself paying attention more to these little moments now, and enjoying the mundane instead of worrying about the bigger picture – and I think this can be a good metaphor for life in general as well.

annotations.comment: Yes! I always say that studying writing has made me both a better and a worse consumer of media. I am more demanding, but also more appreciative. This was a lovely paper, Lincoln, which makes sense because you did excellent work all term. Thank you and happy summer!
